response:
Here is a complete pytest test suite covering all required scenarios, including explicit documentation of this implementation's real-world behaviour:

```python
import pytest
# Replace import below with your actual module location
from your_module import title_case


class TestTitleCaseFunction:
    @pytest.mark.parametrize(
        "input_string, expected_output",
        [
            # ------------------------------
            # ✅ HAPPY PATH CASES
            # ------------------------------
            ("hello world", "Hello World"),
            ("singleword", "Singleword"),
            ("PYTHON UNIT TESTS", "Python Unit Tests"),
            ("hElLo mIxEd cAsE", "Hello Mixed Case"),
            ("Hello Correct Title", "Hello Correct Title"),  # Already properly formatted input

            # ------------------------------
            # ⚠️ EDGE CASES
            # ------------------------------
            ("", ""),  # Empty string input
            ("    ", ""),  # Input contains only spaces
            ("\t\n \r  ", ""),  # Input contains only whitespace control characters
            ("a", "A"),  # Single lowercase character
            ("X", "X"),  # Single uppercase character
            ("multiple     extra    spaces", "Multiple Extra Spaces"),  # Collapsed whitespace
            ("hello\tworld\nnew\rline", "Hello World New Line"),  # Mixed whitespace separators
            ("release v2.4.1 notes", "Release V2.4.1 Notes"),  # Input with numbers/symbols
            ("one two three four five six seven", "One Two Three Four Five Six Seven"),  # Many words
        ],
        ids=lambda val: repr(val)
    )
    def test_valid_inputs_produce_expected_output(self, input_string, expected_output):
        assert title_case(input_string) == expected_output


    @pytest.mark.parametrize(
        "bad_input",
        [
            None,
            12345,
            3.14159,
            ["list", "of", "words"],
            {"dictionary": "value"},
            True,
            bytes("hello", "utf8")
        ]
    )
    def test_non_string_input_raises_type_error(self, bad_input):
        """Verify invalid input types correctly throw expected error"""
        with pytest.raises(TypeError):
            title_case(bad_input)


    def test_implementation_quirks_documentation(self):
        """
        Explicitly documents known limitations of this simple title_case implementation.
        These tests demonstrate what this function *actually does*, not ideal title case behaviour.
        """
        # ❌ This function will NOT correctly format apostrophe proper nouns:
        assert title_case("o'neill") == "O'neill"  # Expected by users: O'Neill

        # ❌ Will NOT format hyphenated compound words:
        assert title_case("hello-world example") == "Hello-world Example"  # Expected: Hello-World Example

        # ❌ Will normalize casing on proper names:
        assert title_case("McDonald") == "Mcdonald"

        # ❌ Will strip all original whitespace formatting:
        assert title_case("   hello   world  ") == "Hello World"
```

---

### Test Coverage Breakdown:
| Category          | Coverage Details |
|-------------------|------------------|
| ✅ Happy Path     | Standard sentences, mixed/upper/lower case input, pre-formatted input |
| ⚠️ Edge Cases     | Empty input, whitespace-only input, single characters, unusual whitespace, values with numbers/symbols |
| ❌ Error Cases    | All common non-string input types, properly verifies expected TypeError |
| 📝 Behaviour Docs | Explicitly tests and documents the known limitations of this simple implementation rather than hiding them |

This suite strictly tests the **exact behaviour** of the function you provided, including implicit behaviour from `str.split()` and `str.capitalize()` rather than testing an ideal title-case standard.
